FT_IC1.evidence: Methods state: 'Cross-sectional study in children of 9-11 years old (n=2,952) and adolescents of 17-18 years old (n=261), from the Dutch ABCD Study and the Spanish INMA Project.' Dutch ABCD = Amsterdam Born Children and their Development — not the U.S. NIH-funded ABCD Study. No U.S. ABCD data analyzed.

notes: Conference abstract in Environmental Epidemiology proceedings. Population is Dutch ABCD Study and Spanish INMA Project — neither is the U.S. ABCD Study. Excluded at FT-IC1 (FT-EC1).

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper states: 'Using survey and interview data collected for 9- to 11-year-olds from the 21-site adolescent brain cognitive development study.' Methods confirm ABCD Study, n=4524 children, data obtained through the National Database for Autism Research / NIMH Data Archive (ABCD data DOI 10.15154/1412097).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis using generalized linear mixed models examining associations between electronic media use and anxiety/depression symptoms in ABCD children.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Child Psychiatry and Human Development (2019); no indication of prior peer-reviewed version in corpus.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 4 (categories of EMU in relationship to anxiety) reports separate coefficients for texting (β=0.021), social networks/SNS (β=0.031, p=0.044), and video chatting (β=0.016) on weekdays, and video chatting on weekends (β=0.038, p=0.013). Social networks (e.g., Twitter, Instagram) and texting are phone/SM-specific variables with their own coefficients, paired with anxiety symptoms (mental health = individual-trait). Table 5 also reports these variables against depression. The STQ captures these SM/phone items separately.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported throughout: standardized β coefficients and t-values in Tables 2, 3, 4, and 5 for all EMU types against anxiety and depression outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis via generalized linear mixed models with numeric results (β, t-value, p, 95% CI) for SM/phone variables (social networks, video chatting, texting) predicting anxiety and depression.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design as condition (a). The paper uses baseline cross-sectional ABCD data only.
notes: EMU categories include texting, social networking sites (Twitter, Instagram), and video chatting measured with the ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ). These qualify as phone/SM-specific variables. Other-side outcomes are anxiety and depression (mental health outcomes = individual-trait). Paper is INCLUDE.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper states: 'Parents and children provided cross-sectional baseline data (2016-18) as part of the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, a broadly US representative sample of 11,875 children aged 9 to 10 years.' Methods reference 21 sites throughout the United States and NIMH Data DOI: 10.15154/1412097.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis using negative binomial structural equation modeling to examine screen time types/content and problem behaviors, with sleep as mediator, in ABCD baseline data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity (2019); no indication of duplicate in corpus.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: Table 2 reports 'social media' as a separate screen time type with its own coefficients against problem behaviors. Social media is defined in methods as comprising texting + visiting social networking sites (e.g., Twitter, Instagram) + video chat (e.g., FaceTime) — exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items. Social media predicts rule-breaking behavior (β=.08, p<.001, IRR=1.084) and aggressive behavior (β=.06, p<.001, IRR=1.062). These are phone/SM-specific estimates paired with behavioral problem syndromes (individual-trait outcomes via CBCL).

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported in Tables 2 and 3: IRRs and β coefficients for social media and all ST types against eight CBCL problem behavior syndromes, plus indirect effects through sleep.

FT_IC4.evidence: Negative binomial SEM with numeric IRRs, β coefficients, p-values, and 95% CIs for SM/phone variable (social media) predicting multiple individual-trait outcomes (CBCL syndromes including aggressive behavior, rule-breaking, etc.).

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; FT-EC7 requires longitudinal design as condition (a). Paper uses baseline cross-sectional ABCD data only.
notes: Social media composite = texting + SNS (Twitter, Instagram) + video chat (FaceTime) — exclusively phone/SM items per methods. Also reports TV/movies, videos, video games, and content (mature-rated games, R-rated movies) separately. Qualifying SM variable has own coefficients against individual-trait outcomes. Paper is INCLUDE.
